mentors.name: Tony Robbins
mentors.bio: World-renowned life and business strategist who has coached Fortune 500 CEOs and world leaders.
mentors.guidance_style: High-energy, pattern-interrupt, focuses on state and strategy

mentors.name: Grant Cardone
mentors.bio: Sales training expert, real estate mogul, and author of The 10X Rule.

mentors.name: Mel Robbins
mentors.bio: Author of The 5 Second Rule, expert on habit formation and overcoming self-doubt.

mentors.name: Codie Sanchez
mentors.bio: Former Wall Street investor turned acquisition entrepreneur. Expert in buying boring businesses.

mentors.name: Richard Branson
mentors.bio: Founder of Virgin Group with 400+ companies. Serial entrepreneur and adventurer.

mentors.name: Chinnakannan Sivasankaran
mentors.bio: Founder of Aircel, built telecom empire in India. Expert in scaling in resource-constrained markets.

mentors.name: Aravind Srinivas
mentors.bio: CEO of Perplexity AI. Expert in building AI-first products and disrupting established markets.

investors.name: Steven Bartlett
investors.bio: Youngest Dragon on Dragons' Den. CEO of Flight Story. Expert in brand identity and social media.
investors.signature_question: What real problem are you solving, and why does it matter now?
investors.walk_out_trigger: Identity unclear - founder doesn't know who they are or why they're doing this

investors.name: Kevin O'Leary
investors.bio: Mr. Wonderful. Chairman of O'Leary Financial Group. Cold, numbers-driven investor.
investors.signature_question: How will you use the capital in the next 12 months?
investors.walk_out_trigger: Financial logic weak - numbers don't add up or founder can't explain unit economics

investors.name: Daymond John
investors.bio: Founder of FUBU. The People's Shark. Expert in brand building from nothing.
investors.signature_question: If a competitor copies you tomorrow, what do you do first?
investors.walk_out_trigger: Brand weak - no clear differentiation or brand strategy

investors.name: Barbara Corcoran
investors.bio: Built The Corcoran Group from a $1,000 loan. Trusts gut instinct and founder grit.
investors.signature_question: Tell me about a moment this business almost failed.
investors.walk_out_trigger: Emotional authenticity weak - founder seems rehearsed or lacks genuine passion

investors.name: Mark Cuban
investors.bio: Owner of Dallas Mavericks. Serial tech entrepreneur. Values product excellence and hustle.
investors.signature_question: What is your biggest blind spot right now?
investors.walk_out_trigger: Scalability weak - product or business model can't scale

investors.name: Lori Greiner
investors.bio: Queen of QVC. Holds 120+ patents. Expert in product-market fit and retail strategy.
investors.signature_question: What is your unfair advantage and how long will it last?
investors.walk_out_trigger: Clarity weak - founder can't clearly explain their product or strategy

investors.name: Robert Herjavec
investors.bio: Founder of Herjavec Group. Immigrant success story. Values integrity and trust above all.
investors.signature_question: Why should I trust you beyond the numbers?
investors.walk_out_trigger: Trust weak - inconsistencies in story or founder seems untrustworthy

leaders.name: Indira Nooyi
leaders.bio: Former CEO of PepsiCo. Known for Performance with Purpose strategy.

leaders.name: Jack Ma
leaders.specialization: Grit, belief under doubt, unconventional thinking
leaders.bio: Founder of Alibaba. Rejected from 30 jobs, built the world's largest e-commerce platform.

leaders.name: Simon Sinek
leaders.bio: Author of Start With Why. Expert in leadership and purpose-driven organizations.

leaders.name: Brian Tracy
leaders.bio: Author of Eat That Frog. Expert in personal productivity and goal achievement.

leaders.name: John C. Maxwell
leaders.bio: Author of The 21 Irrefutable Laws of Leadership. World's foremost leadership expert.

leaders.name: Anand Mahindra
leaders.bio: Chairman of Mahindra Group. Known for patient capital and nation-building vision.

leaders.name: Sridhar Vembu
leaders.bio: Founder and CEO of Zoho. Bootstrapped to $1B+ revenue without external funding.

competencies.name: Problem Sensing
competencies.description: Does the founder deeply understand the customer's real pain?
what_it_measures: Solves meaningful problems, not surface inconveniences
Articulates emotional + financial cost of the problem
Obsessed with the problem before the solution
developing: Talks more about solution than customer pain
Cannot clearly articulate emotional + financial cost
Focuses on idea excitement over problem obsession
strong: Can clearly describe customer pain and stakes
Balances problem depth with solution development
advanced: Deeply obsessed with understanding root pain
Quantifies emotional and financial impact clearly
Anticipates adjacent and second-order customer problems
Builds solution from pain precision, not assumption

competencies.name: Learning Agility
competencies.description: Does the founder actively seek feedback and adapt?
what_it_measures: Willing to pivot based on learning
advanced: Evolves model ahead of market shifts

competencies.name: Courage to Commit
competencies.description: Can the founder make decisions with incomplete information?
developing: Hesitates to take or own risk
strong: Makes timely decisions with imperfect info

competencies.name: Financial Discipline
competencies.description: Does the founder understand and manage money wisely?
developing: Uses hiring to compensate for weak strategy
strong: Uses partnerships to offset cash constraints
Calculates equity trade against dilution impact

competencies.name: Strategic Thinking
competencies.description: Can the founder see the big picture beyond day-to-day operations?
what_it_measures: Sees big picture beyond daily ops
Understands how today's decisions impact 1-3 years ahead
Fixes root causes instead of patches
developing: Struggles to connect decisions to long-term impact
Reacts to problems instead of anticipating
strong: Pauses before reacting to external shocks

competencies.name: Power & Influence
competencies.description: Can the founder defend value and negotiate from strength?
developing: Accepts unfavorable terms to avoid discomfort
strong: Protects core terms while remaining flexible
advanced: Practices ABC mindset and takes quick win-win decisions

competencies.name: Team & Customer Management
competencies.description: Does the founder genuinely care about customer satisfaction and team well-being?
what_it_measures: Genuinely cares about customer satisfaction beyond transactions
Treats team as assets to grow, not exploit
Makes decisions with long-term trust in mind
Builds feedback loops for customers and employees
strong: Checks bias in hiring and promotions
advanced: Protects both customer and team equity under pressure
Prioritizes humanity over profits when demanded
Maintains psychological safety under performance pressure

competencies.name: Value Creation & Personal Credibility
competencies.description: Does the founder build lasting value and reputation?
what_it_measures: Considers impact on family, team, community
Builds brand equity, not just numbers
strong: Actively shares knowledge and educates customers
advanced: Operates as value creator first, revenue follows
Invests in community, team growth, and ecosystem
Builds authority through service and insight

questions.text: What is your business idea in one clear sentence?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate clarity, specificity, and problem-focus of this business idea statement.

questions.text: What specific problem does it solve?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate if the founder identifies a real pain point vs surface inconvenience. Look for emotional + financial cost articulation.

questions.text: Who is your target audience? Be as specific as possible (demographics, psychographics, context).
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate specificity of target customer definition. Look for demographics, psychographics, and context.

questions.text: Why would someone pay for this?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate understanding of willingness to pay. Does founder understand value vs cost?

questions.text: Is this a NEED or a WANT? And Why?

options.text: A critical need - people are actively suffering without a solution
A strong want - people desire this and would pay for convenience
Somewhere between - it depends on the customer
More of a nice-to-have but the market is big

questions.text: How are people currently solving this problem?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate competitive awareness. Does founder understand existing solutions and why they're insufficient?

questions.text: How will this business make money? (Product / Service / Subscription / Other)

options.text: One-time product sale
Recurring subscription/membership
Service (per-project or hourly)
Marketplace/platform (commission-based)
Freemium with premium upsell
Not sure yet
options.warning: No revenue model is a red flag

questions.text: What will you charge? Why that price?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate pricing rationale. Look for value-based thinking, competitor awareness, and willingness-to-pay research.

questions.text: Roughly how much will it cost to launch the first version?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate cost awareness and realism. Does founder understand bootstrapping vs over-investing?

questions.text: What is the biggest risk or weakness in this idea?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate self-awareness and ability to identify genuine risks vs surface-level concerns. P3 if founder names non-obvious structural risk.

questions.text: In ONE sentence: What is your business vision? What does success look like in 12 months?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate vision clarity, realism, and long-term thinking.

questions.text: How will your business be positioned differently from competitors in 1-3 years?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate strategic positioning and long-term brand thinking.

questions.text: 3 experienced entrepreneurs review your idea. They say: 1) Market is saturated, 2) Customers lack budget, 3) Hard execution. How do you respond?
questions.pressure_text: Your vision is being challenged by experienced voices.

options.text: Stick with my plan - I believe in it
Ask follow-up questions to understand their perspective deeper
Pivot to a different idea
Acknowledge valid points and modify my approach

questions.text: What kind of leader do you want to be known as? What values will your company stand for?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate depth of values thinking and personal credibility vision.

questions.text: You have limited capital. How will you fund your launch?

options.text: Bootstrap with personal savings
Take a business loan
Seek angel investors
Pre-sell to customers before building
Find a co-founder who invests
options.signal: Team strength but high equity cost

options.text: Wait until I have more savings

questions.text: An experienced advisor tells you: 'Don't launch yet. Do 3 more months of research.' But you feel ready. What do you do?
questions.pressure_text: An authority figure is telling you to wait.

options.text: Follow the advisor's suggestion and wait
Launch a basic MVP now while doing research in parallel
Launch immediately, full product
Ask the advisor what specific research would change my approach

questions.text: You have your starting capital. Allocate it across these categories (must total 100%):

options.text: Product Development (recommended: 30-50%)
Marketing & Sales (recommended: 15-40%)
Hiring (recommended: 0-30%)
Operations (recommended: 5-15%)
Emergency Buffer (recommended: 10-25%)

warnings.hiring_gt_30: WARNING: >30% on hiring before PMF = risky
warnings.buffer_lt_10: WARNING: Low buffer could derail you
warnings.product_lt_25: WARNING: Low product budget = quality issues

questions.text: With your budget, you can: A) Ship a basic MVP in 1 month, or B) Build a polished product in 3 months. Which do you choose?

options.text: Ship basic MVP in 1 month - learn from real customers
Build polished product in 3 months - quality matters
options.warning: 3 months with no revenue is risky

questions.text: A friend (talented designer) offers to work for 10% equity instead of $15K cash. What do you do?

options.text: Accept - equity is cheaper than cash right now
Negotiate 5% with 4-year vesting schedule
Offer profit-sharing instead of equity
Decline - I'll do the design myself

questions.text: Product is LIVE! Month 3. You have zero customers. How do you get your first 10?

options.text: Cold outreach - email/DM potential customers directly
Run paid ads on social media
Leverage personal network and ask for referrals
Partner with complementary businesses for cross-promotion
Create valuable content to attract inbound interest

questions.text: Month 3: You got 8 customers! But 2 request refunds saying 'not what I expected.' Meanwhile, 3 others say they love it but want Feature X. What is your Month 4 priority?
questions.pressure_text: Low traction and early churn are testing your resolve.

options.text: Focus on retention - talk to the 2 churning customers to understand why
Double down on acquisition - get more customers
Build Feature X immediately - 3 customers asked for it
Talk to ALL customers (happy and unhappy) to understand patterns

questions.text: A customer posts a negative review publicly: 'Terrible product. Waste of money. Don't buy.' You have 15 other happy customers. How do you respond?
questions.pressure_text: Your reputation is being attacked publicly.

options.text: Respond publicly with empathy, offer to make it right
Ignore it - one bad review won't matter
Contact them privately first, then respond publicly
Ask happy customers to post positive reviews to counter it

questions.text: After 2 months of sales, what have you LEARNED that you didn't expect? What would you change about your product or approach?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate learning agility. Does founder show genuine learning from market feedback? Do they separate ego from iteration?

questions.text: Month 5: Revenue is growing 20% month-over-month. You're working 70+ hours/week. You can afford to hire ONE person. What role?

options.text: Customer support person - customers need faster responses
Sales person - to accelerate revenue
Operations/automation - build systems first, then scale people
Don't hire yet - build systems and automation instead
Co-founder/partner for equity - can't afford salary

questions.text: Month 6: Cash flow crunch. Revenue is $15K/mo but expenses are $12K/mo. A client offers $50K for a custom project that would take 2 months and derail your product roadmap. What do you do?
questions.pressure_text: The cash is tempting but the opportunity cost is real.

options.text: Take it - the cash injection is critical
Decline - stay focused on the product roadmap
Negotiate: $75K, extended timeline, hire contractor to handle it
options.signal: Creative, protects core while capturing value

options.text: Accept but only if it can lead to recurring revenue from that client

questions.text: Your first employee (3 months in) is underperforming. They're a good person but output is 50% of what you expected. Team is just 2 people. What do you do?
questions.pressure_text: Team burnout is escalating. You can't do everything alone.

options.text: Fire them quickly - you can't afford dead weight
Have an honest conversation: understand WHY, set clear expectations with timeline
Give them easier tasks while you handle the hard stuff
Create proper onboarding, documentation, and check if YOUR systems are the bottleneck

questions.text: Month 8: A larger company offers a strategic partnership. They'll send you 200 customers BUT demand exclusive rights to your technology in their industry and 15% equity. What do you do?
questions.pressure_text: A misaligned partnership offer with high upside and high cost.

options.text: Accept - 200 customers would be transformative
options.signal: Gives up leverage for short-term gain

options.text: Negotiate: non-exclusive, 5% equity, revenue-share instead
Decline - the equity and exclusivity cost is too high
Counter: time-limited exclusive (1 year), no equity, performance-based partnership

questions.text: Month 9: A competitor launches a similar product at 50% your price. 3 of your customers churn. What's your first move?
questions.pressure_text: An undercutting competitor is stealing your customers.

options.text: Match their pricing to stay competitive
Double down on value - add features and services they can't match
Talk to the churned customers to understand EXACTLY why they switched
Accelerate product roadmap to create features they can't copy

questions.text: Month 10: You receive an acquisition offer of $2M. Your annual revenue is $180K. The acquiring company is reputable. What do you do?

options.text: Accept - $2M is life-changing money
Negotiate for higher valuation and better terms
Decline - I believe this business is worth much more in 2-3 years
Explore a strategic partnership instead of full acquisition

questions.text: Month 10: Your best employee (6 months, key relationships) says they're leaving for a competitor offering 40% more salary. Team morale is shaken. What do you do?
questions.pressure_text: A key team member is quitting. Team morale is at risk.

options.text: Match the offer to keep them
Have an honest conversation - understand what they really want beyond money
Let them go - no one is irreplaceable
Offer equity/growth path + moderate raise, show the long-term vision

questions.text: Month 11: A customer posts a viral social media thread attacking your company's credibility. Claims are partially true (slow support times) but exaggerated. 50K views. What do you do?
questions.pressure_text: A social media credibility attack is going viral.

options.text: Issue a defensive public statement refuting the claims
Acknowledge the valid criticism publicly, share specific fix plan with timeline
Reach out privately to resolve, then post update
Ignore it and focus on fixing the underlying issue

questions.text: Month 11: Revenue dipped 15% this month. Economy is slowing. Team of 4 is anxious. You have 4 months of runway. How do you handle this?
questions.pressure_text: Revenue dip + economic slowdown + anxious team.

options.text: Cut 2 team members immediately to extend runway
Reduce your own salary first, then discuss with team transparently
Call emergency meeting - be transparent, involve team in cost-cutting decisions
Don't tell the team - you'll figure it out and don't want to cause panic

questions.text: Looking back at your 11-month journey so far: What is the most important lesson you've learned about YOURSELF as a leader?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate depth of self-reflection, learning integration, and growth mindset. P3 if founder demonstrates genuine self-awareness about specific behavioral patterns.

questions.text: You're about to enter the War Room. In 2-3 sentences, what is your valuation and how do you justify it?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate financial logic, confidence, and narrative strength. P3 if founder uses numbers strategically with compelling growth story.

questions.text: What is your capital ask? How much are you raising and for what percentage of equity? How will you use the funds?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate clarity of capital allocation plan, equity protection awareness, and strategic use of funds.

questions.text: What is the ONE thing that makes your business UNSTOPPABLE in the next 3 years?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate strategic vision and credibility of growth narrative. P3 if founder articulates genuine competitive moat.

questions.text:
Deliver your 1-minute War Room pitch. Use this structure:

Hello Sharks, my name is [NAME] and I am the founder of [BUSINESS].

(The Problem) Today, [TARGET CUSTOMER] struggles with [PROBLEM]. This problem causes them [IMPACT].

(The Solution) I created [PRODUCT], which [VALUE PROP]. It works by [HOW].

(Why We're Different) Unlike [COMPETITORS], we [DIFFERENTIATION].

(Proof) We validated this by [VALIDATION]. So far, we have [TRACTION].

(Founder Fit) I am building this because [WHY ME]. The key lesson I've learned is [LESSON].

(The Ask) We are raising $[AMOUNT] for [EQUITY]% equity. We will use this capital to [PLAN].
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate pitch completeness, clarity, confidence, financial logic, and overall narrative strength. This is the final integrated assessment.
